bull_thesis: ABB is a wide-moat electrification compounder sitting in the demand sweet spot of the AI build-out: low- and medium-voltage switchgear, medium-voltage uninterruptible power supplies (UPS), DC distribution, drives, and grid-grade power equipment — the physical electrical layer every data-centre operator and grid-interconnection project must buy through. The first quarter of 2026 marks a genuine inflection: record order intake above $11.3 billion (up 24% on a comparable basis, up 32% as reported), a book-to-bill ratio (orders received divided by revenue shipped) of 1.29, data-centre orders up triple digits year on year with records set in both the Electrification and Motion divisions, and an operational earnings margin of 23.5% — above the top of the freshly-upgraded 18–22% target range. Management raised full-year 2026 comparable-revenue guidance from 6–9% growth to high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth, and guided margin higher year on year even after stripping out a $425 million real-estate gain. The 1.29 book-to-bill already underwrites most of that revenue. The franchise quality stands out: return on invested capital of roughly 16%, rising toward a return-on-capital-employed target above 20%, net debt roughly 0.3 times earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA), and free-cash-flow conversion above 95%. Capital allocation is an additional tailwind: a $2.0 billion share-buyback programme running through January 2027, plus the agreed sale of the Robotics division to SoftBank, delivering roughly $5.3 billion in net cash and a roughly $2.4 billion pre-tax gain in the second half of 2026 — a clean exit from a non-core unit that adds substantial balance-sheet firepower for further buybacks and bolt-on acquisitions. A collaboration with NVIDIA on 800-volt DC power architecture (announced October 2025, white paper published in the first quarter of 2026) positions ABB's medium-voltage UPS and solid-state power electronics inside the gigawatt-scale data-centre power stack. ABB is a rising-returns, expanding-margin, widening-moat beneficiary of electrification; the secular shift is a tailwind rather than a threat. Key conditions for maintaining this view: data-centre and grid order durability through the 2026–28 backlog conversion, no large pull-forward unwind, and the margin target sustained excluding one-off items.
bear_thesis: The operational news is excellent, but it is almost entirely already reflected in the share price. At roughly $108 as of 18 June 2026, ABB trades at approximately 33 times forward earnings and 39 times trailing earnings — at or above Eaton (roughly 32 times forward) and Schneider Electric (roughly 30 times forward). In other words, the AI electrification optionality is fully embedded in a stock that has already risen roughly 87% over the trailing year. The load-bearing assumption — that data-centre and grid orders stay strong — is the same shared macroeconomic factor (AI capital-spending durability) driving the entire scale-out Basket B and, according to the regime analysis, roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth. ABB adds correlated, not independent, exposure to a book that already leans heavily on one wall. Record electrical-equipment orders late in a capital-spending cycle carry classic double-ordering, pull-forward, and scarcity-booking risk: a single hyperscaler capital-spending pause would convert a book-to-bill of 1.29 into a backlog drain and a sharp multiple de-rating, because nothing in a 33-times multiple prices in any deceleration. The first-quarter free-cash-flow headline is also flattered by that $425 million one-off real-estate gain. The NVIDIA collaboration is a credible architecture specification, not a binding multi-year supply contract with a disclosed dollar value — the revenue it represents is optionality, not contracted business. In short: a great company at a price that demands the AI build-out compounds uninterrupted. The asymmetry is unfavourable because the upside is largely realised and the downside — a cohort-wide de-rating on any capital-spending scare — is not protected against. The key falsification signals to watch are a sequential data-centre order deceleration, the book-to-bill slipping toward 1.0, or a hyperscaler capital-spending guidance reduction.
sop_bull: Valuing the whole company (with Robotics now a forthcoming cash event rather than an operating segment). Core ABB on roughly $35 billion of estimated 2026 revenue: the Electrification segment (roughly 50% of revenue and the DC and grid engine) deserves a premium multiple of roughly 20 times segment earnings given above-20% order growth and record intake; Motion (roughly 30%) roughly 16 times; Process Automation (roughly 20%) roughly 14 times. Blending those and applying a peer-justified roughly 24 times forward earnings to an estimated 2026 net income of $5.2–5.5 billion (assuming the margin target is sustained at the top of the 18–22% range and data-centre mix remains accretive) supports an equity value of roughly $125–135 billion from the operating core alone. Adding special-situation overlays — the SoftBank Robotics net cash proceeds of roughly $5.3 billion plus the roughly $2.4 billion pre-tax gain, plus the net-cash balance-sheet position post-closing, plus the $2.0 billion buyback reducing the share count — the sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) bull case lands at roughly $125–135 per depositary receipt, implying mid-to-high teens upside from the roughly $108 starting point if the data-centre supercycle continues and the cash is returned to shareholders.
sop_bear: Taking a conservative whole-company view and normalising for a post-pull-forward cycle: apply an in-line-to-modest-discount multiple of roughly 22–24 times to a haircut net income of $4.7–4.9 billion (assuming the book-to-bill reverts toward 1.0, data-centre orders decelerate, and margin holds at roughly 20% rather than 23.5%). That produces operating equity of roughly $105–112 billion. The Robotics cash (roughly $5.3 billion) and the net-cash balance sheet are real and additive regardless of the cycle. But strip out the AI premium and a cyclical electrical-equipment franchise at a market-average 18–20 times on trough-ish earnings clears at roughly $80–90 per depositary receipt. The sum-of-the-parts bear case is therefore roughly $85–92 per depositary receipt — implying roughly 15–25% downside if the cohort de-rates, with the Robotics cash and fortress balance sheet providing a floor.
sop_breakdown: Operating core (three segments, blended roughly 23–24 times forward on estimated 2026 net income of $5.0–5.2 billion, with Electrification carrying the premium for above-20% data-centre and grid growth): roughly $92–98 per depositary receipt. Adding the net-cash and fortress balance-sheet value (net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA): roughly $4–6 per depositary receipt of optionality. Adding the SoftBank Robotics sale (roughly $5.3 billion net cash plus roughly $2.4 billion pre-tax gain, mid-to-late 2026, verified — a pivot from the abandoned second-quarter 2026 spin or initial public offering plan): roughly $3–4 per depositary receipt once received, partly funding the $2.0 billion buyback. Base-case sum-of-the-parts: roughly $100–108 per depositary receipt — essentially at the roughly $108 spot price. The stock is close to fairly valued to slightly rich on a sober blended multiple. The bull needs the data-centre supercycle to keep compounding to justify a print above $125; the bear needs a capital-spending air-pocket to open the $85–92 downside.
risk_reward: Roughly symmetric to unfavourable from the roughly $108 level. Upside to the bull fair-value estimate (roughly $130) is approximately 20%; downside to the bear break (roughly $88) is approximately 19%. The base-case fair value (roughly $104) sits below the current price. With the catalysts — record orders, the guidance raise, the NVIDIA architecture specification — already reflected in the price and the multiple at the top of the peer group, the asymmetry does not favour a fresh, aggressive entry. The Robotics cash and the balance sheet cap the downside, but the AI optionality is largely realised.
catalyst_status: FIRED. The load-bearing catalysts have already printed and re-rated the stock: (1) record Q1-2026 orders >$11.3B + book-to-bill 1.29 + FY26 guidance RAISE (Apr-22-2026) — public, already in the +87% trailing-year move; (2) NVIDIA 800 VDC collaboration announced Oct-2025 with the Q1-2026 white paper delivered — a spec-in, not a dated binding award, so SOFT on the contract dimension but already known to the market; (3) SoftBank Robotics sale (~$5.3B) is PENDING_HARD on timing (closes mid-to-late 2026) but the value is largely known and the use-of-proceeds (buyback) already disclosed. Net classification: FIRED / already-ran — the re-rate is spent; only the Robotics close is a forward dated event, and it is an arb-grade cash receipt, not an asymmetric surprise.
peer_comps_note: The true peer group for the AI data-centre electrical-power layer comprises Eaton (ETN, roughly 31.8 times forward as of 19 June 2026), Schneider Electric (roughly 29.7 times forward), Vertiv (a pure-play data-centre power and cooling name, richer at roughly 35–45 times given roughly 30% revenue growth and roughly $9.5 billion of backlog), and Siemens Energy (grid and high-voltage transformers, separately listed). ABB (roughly 33 times forward, roughly 39 times trailing; market cap roughly $196 billion at $108 per depositary receipt) ranks as follows across the relevant dimensions. On valuation: rich — at or above Eaton and Schneider, below pure-play Vertiv. On growth: strong (record orders, book-to-bill 1.29, data-centre orders up triple digits) but a notch below Vertiv's pure-play data-centre growth rate. On margins: best-in-class at 23.5% operational earnings margin in the first quarter of 2026 against Schneider and Eaton in the high teens, and well above Vertiv's operating margin. On returns and balance-sheet quality: elite — return on invested capital roughly 16% rising toward a return-on-capital-employed target above 20%, net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA — the cleanest balance sheet in the group. On AI capital-spending exposure: broad and durable (grid plus data-centre plus drives) but more diversified and therefore less levered to data-centre pure-play than Vertiv, which is the closest comparable. In summary: ABB is the highest-quality, best-balance-sheet name in the cohort, priced accordingly. There is no valuation discount to capture; the entire group has re-rated together on the shared AI capital-spending factor.
role_in_scaleout: ABB supplies the physical electrical distribution layer the AI build-out cannot bypass — low- and medium-voltage switchgear, medium-voltage UPS, DC distribution, and solid-state power electronics (specified into NVIDIA's 800-volt DC gigawatt-rack architecture) together with grid-interconnection equipment and drives. This positions ABB as critical, scale-cost-moated infrastructure. Demand durability is high through the 2026–28 grid and data-centre backlog, but the driver is the same shared AI capital-spending factor as the rest of Basket B — durable and cohort-correlated rather than idiosyncratic.
consensus_delta: There is no classic 'where we disagree with the market' long opportunity here — the street is already looking the right way and has paid for the view (forward multiple roughly 33 times, trailing-year gain roughly 87%, the full-year 2026 guidance already raised). The only genuine expectations gap runs on the bear side: the street is extrapolating record orders (book-to-bill 1.29) as a durable run-rate while embedding little risk of late-cycle double-ordering or scarcity-booking in electrical equipment, and is treating the data-centre order surge as structural demand rather than partly a pull-forward of scarce switchgear and transformers. The implicit street assumption is that a 33-times multiple is justified because data-centre and grid capital spending compounds uninterrupted — yet roughly 40% of 2026 S&P 500 earnings-per-share growth (per the regime analysis) leans on that same AI capital-spending wall, making this a crowded, cohort-correlated bet rather than a differentiated mispricing. If there is an arbitrage, it lies in recognising that the good news is fully in the price and the asymmetry has inverted: the next surprise likely to move the stock is a capital-spending digestion downside, not an upside the market has not already underwritten.
valley_of_death: ABB has no balance-sheet danger zone — net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA, free cash flow strong (roughly $1.3 billion in the first quarter, though $425 million was a one-off real-estate gain), $5.3 billion of SoftBank cash on the way, and no near-term debt-maturity wall. The risk over the next three to nine months is entirely a multiple and macroeconomic one, not a solvency one. Three specific scenarios create that risk window: first, any hyperscaler capital-spending guidance reduction or AI-monetisation scare — the regime analysis explicitly flags AI capital spending as the dominant tripwire, responsible for roughly 40% of 2026 earnings-per-share growth — would trigger broad de-grossing of the grid and data-centre cohort, and a 33-times-forward name has the furthest to fall; second, a sequential data-centre order deceleration or the book-to-bill slipping toward 1.0 in the next one or two quarterly prints would puncture the 'durable record orders' narrative; third, the broader market backdrop is late-cycle and complacent (Bank of America Fund Manager Survey cash at 3.9%, a recognised sell signal; the seven largest US technology stocks at roughly 30% of the index; live inflation and oil tails) with limited dry powder to absorb a shock. The Robotics closing (mid-to-late 2026) is a positive cash event, but execution and regulatory-approval risk is non-zero. None of these scenarios impair the franchise; all of them can hit the price hard before any new positive catalyst arrives.
positioning_washout: There is no forced-selling dynamic and no dividend-cut risk — quite the opposite. ABB is a quality-momentum holding owned by growth, quality, and industrial-electrification funds as well as AI-thematic baskets. The roughly 0.9% dividend yield means there is no yield-focused shareholder base that would be forced out, and the $2.0 billion buyback plus the Robotics cash add a structural bid beneath the shares. The positioning risk runs in the other direction: ABB is a crowded, fully-owned, fully-valued momentum name. The meaningful selling that matters would be a broad AI capital-spending de-grossing event — the regime analysis flags this as its book-independence concern — in which ABB sells off alongside the cohort regardless of its own fundamentals. There is no idiosyncratic forced-selling pressure to buy into; there is a cohort-wide flush to wait for.
forcing_function: The re-rating catalyst has already fired (first-quarter 2026 results and the guidance raise, 22 April 2026). The remaining dated events that could force another re-rating are: first, second-quarter 2026 results (expected late July 2026) — the first read on whether the record book-to-bill and data-centre orders are sustaining or decelerating (the key falsification point for the bull case); second, the SoftBank Robotics deal close (mid-to-late 2026), which crystallises roughly $5.3 billion in cash plus roughly $2.4 billion in pre-tax gain, likely accelerating the buyback; third, any hyperscaler 2026 or 2027 capital-spending revision during earnings season — the macro forcing function for the entire cohort. None of these represents an obviously positive, asymmetric surprise. The second-quarter print is more a downside risk (potential deceleration) than an upside catalyst, given how much has already been priced in.
moderator_conclusion: ABB is the highest-quality name in scale-out Basket B (the grid sleeve): the moat is wide and widening — return on invested capital at roughly 16% rising toward a return-on-capital-employed target above 20%, operational earnings margin of 23.5% above an upgraded 18–22% target range — the balance sheet is a fortress (net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA) and is about to be reinforced by roughly $5.3 billion of SoftBank Robotics cash, and the company has a genuine, critical role in the AI electrical build-out (switchgear, medium-voltage UPS, DC distribution, the NVIDIA 800-volt DC architecture specification, plus grid interconnection and drives). Forensic quality rates 4 out of 5, trajectory strengthening. But the regime overlay and the price both argue against an aggressive entry. The load-bearing catalysts have already fired: record orders, the full-year 2026 guidance raise, and the NVIDIA collaboration are all public and already embedded in a roughly 87% trailing-year gain at roughly 33 times forward earnings — at or above Eaton (roughly 32 times) and Schneider (roughly 30 times), with no valuation discount to capture versus a peer group that has re-rated together. Applying the catalyst-watch regime framework: this is a late-cycle, complacent market environment (Fund Manager Survey cash at 3.9%, the seven largest US technology stocks at roughly 30% of the index, live oil and inflation tails) whose single dominant factor is AI capital-spending durability — exactly the factor ABB's thesis depends on, so it fails the test for whether the return driver is independent from the rest of the book (it adds correlated, not orthogonal, exposure to a portfolio that already leans on this one wall). The regime analysis explicitly flags an AI capital-spending break as the de-grossing tripwire, and a 33-times-forward name is most exposed in a digestion air-pocket. Recommendation: wait for the wash-out. The franchise is real and worth owning, but the asymmetry is currently unfavourable and the catalyst is spent. Wait for a specific flush — a broad AI capital-spending de-grossing event or a sequential data-centre order deceleration (book-to-bill trending toward 1.0, or a below-consensus second-quarter print around late July) that resets the multiple from the top of the peer group toward the low-to-mid $90s, where the elite balance sheet, the Robotics cash, and the widening moat would offer a defensible entry. Conviction 3. On a pure value lens (setting aside catalyst and regime), the base-case sum-of-the-parts of roughly $100–108 sits at spot, so even a high-quality compounder offers no margin of safety here; a wide-moat name at a full multiple is a hold, not a buy. The secular force — electrification — is a tailwind, and the secular threat is manageable, so no structural ceiling on the position is warranted.
interrogator_dossier:
Forensic Dossier — ABB Ltd (NYSE depositary receipt: ABBNY / SIX: ABBN)

Prepared as the independent interrogator. Data verified June 2026 from ABB's first-quarter 2026 results release (22 April 2026), the fourth-quarter 2025 results, the 2025 Capital Markets Day, and market data (price $108.47, 18 June 2026; market cap roughly $196 billion; trailing price-to-earnings roughly 39 times, forward roughly 33 times; dividend yield roughly 0.9%). No bundled transcript set was supplied; this synthesises the most recent reported quarters from public materials. Where a multi-quarter quoted arc cannot be sourced from public filings, that is stated rather than assumed.

Narrative Arc
ABB's story has shifted over roughly six quarters from a margin self-help and portfolio-pruning narrative (the 'ABB Way' decentralisation, disciplined capital allocation, acquisition hurdle-rate discipline) into a demand-led electrification supercycle narrative whose single loudest driver is now AI data-centre power. At the 2025 Capital Markets Day, management upgraded the through-cycle targets — operational earnings margin to 18–22%, return on capital employed to above 20%, free-cash-flow conversion above 95% — an explicit acknowledgement that the franchise had re-rated its own earnings power. By the first quarter of 2026 the narrative is unambiguously offensive: record order intake above $11.3 billion (up 24–32% comparable and reported), book-to-bill of 1.29, data-centre orders up triple digits year on year, records in both Electrification and Motion. The portfolio-simplification thread reached its conclusion with the decision to sell Robotics to SoftBank (roughly $5.3 billion net cash, roughly $2.4 billion pre-tax gain, mid-to-late 2026) — abandoning the earlier 2025 plan to spin off or float Robotics in the second quarter of 2026. Quietly dropped: the standalone Robotics initial public offering equity story, replaced by a cleaner cash sale. Newly amplified: the NVIDIA 800-volt DC collaboration (October 2025, first-quarter 2026 white paper) on the medium-voltage UPS, DC distribution, and solid-state side of the gigawatt power stack.

Claims vs Financials
Record orders and broadening demand: verified. Orders $11.3 billion, up 24% on a comparable basis; book-to-bill 1.29; Electrification orders $6.6 billion, up 44%. Data centres as the strongest market: verified as an order and backlog story, not yet a dominant revenue line. Data-centre orders up triple digits, records in both Electrification and Motion. Margin algorithm structurally higher (18–22%): the company is over-delivering — first-quarter 2026 operational earnings margin 23.5%, above the top of the target range — though the result is flattered by the $425 million real-estate gain in free cash flow and by seasonal mix. Free-cash-flow conversion above 95%: verified with an asterisk — first-quarter free cash flow roughly $1.3 billion, approximately double the prior year, including the $425 million one-off. Fortress balance sheet: verified. Net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA; return on invested capital roughly 16%; return on capital employed at an all-time high. Disciplined capital allocation: verified. $2.0 billion buyback to January 2027, CHF 0.94 dividend, plus the incoming Robotics cash. Net assessment: the narrative is conservative in one respect (it undersells how far the margin beat the target range) and generous in another (the data-centre framing implies a bigger revenue base than currently exists).

Tone and Confidence Trajectory
Rising. Full-year 2026 comparable-revenue guidance lifted from 6–9% to high-single-digit to low-double-digit growth, with margin guided higher year on year even after stripping out the real-estate gain. Language moved from hedged to declarative — 'record', 'strongest in data centres', 'broad-based'. Tone is offensive (orders, capacity, AI build-out) rather than defensive (cost-cutting, destocking). Residual caution applies only to short-cycle industrial and tariff-sensitive demand.

Guidance Credibility
High. The pattern has been to meet or beat the margin walk and then raise the structural target (as at the 2025 Capital Markets Day). The first-quarter 2026 guidance raise is backed by a hard book-to-bill of 1.29, reducing the hope content. The credibility risk is transparency around one-off items (the $425 million real-estate gain in the free-cash-flow headline), not delivery. Reliability: high.

Analyst Pressure Points
(1) Durability of the data-centre order surge versus pull-forward or double-ordering (late-cycle electrical-equipment risk). (2) Backlog and pricing quality at peak volumes. (3) Deployment of the $5.3 billion Robotics proceeds. (4) Short-cycle industrial softness in Europe and China potentially masked by long-cycle data-centre and grid strength. Management leans on backlog breadth and grid (not just data-centre) demand as the answer — credible, but not fully falsifiable until the cycle turns.

Red Flags and Green Flags
On the positive side: book-to-bill 1.29, with orders roughly twice revenue — backlog building. Net debt roughly 0.3 times EBITDA, plus roughly $5.3 billion of SoftBank cash inbound. Margin algorithm (18–22%) being exceeded (23.5% printed). NVIDIA 800-volt DC architecture specification. Robotics sold for cash and a gain rather than via a weak-window initial public offering. On the cautionary side: the $425 million real-estate gain inflating first-quarter free cash flow. Valuation at roughly 33 times forward and roughly 39 times trailing — AI optionality largely priced in. Data-centre order durability is the load-bearing, cohort-shared assumption across Schneider, Siemens Energy, Eaton, and Vertiv — correlated, not idiosyncratic.

Hidden Signals
The pivot from a Robotics spin-off to a cash sale signals management prioritises clean balance-sheet firepower over equity-stub engineering, and implicitly that the standalone Robotics story was not strong enough for a premium public float. The repeated pairing of 'Electrification AND Motion records' signals the data-centre theme now pulls two segments — power and drives for cooling and airflow. The explicit 'margin higher even excluding the real-estate gain' guidance pre-empts the bear's one-off objection — a confidence signal worth noting.

Capital-Allocation Assessment
Classification: strengthening. High-quality, under-levered industrial compounder in the demand sweet spot of the AI build-out, executing against an upgraded-and-over-delivered margin algorithm, monetising a non-core unit for $5.3 billion, and returning cash via a $2 billion buyback. The single most important thing to weigh: how much AI capital-spending optionality is already in the roughly 33 times forward multiple. The operational news is excellent but the entry price is rich and the load-bearing catalyst — data-centre order durability — is the same shared macroeconomic factor as the rest of Basket B. Moat sources: scale cost, installed base, switching costs, specification and certification barriers, global service. Moat width: wide and widening — return on invested capital roughly 16% rising toward a return on capital employed above 20%, with operational margin progressing from 15% to 18–22% to a printed 23.5%, the signature of a widening moat. Secular threat: none to manageable — ABB is a beneficiary of electrification; the only terminal risk is cyclical (a post-AI-capex digestion air-pocket), which is a multiple and timing risk, not a structural impairment. The risk here is the opposite of a value trap: the danger is paying top dollar at the peak of a cohort that has run hard.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; moat wide; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
skeptic_kill_fact: No fatal flaw found. The bear case — excellent fundamentals but fully priced in, a cohort-correlated AI capital-spending bet, the catalyst already fired, and asymmetry unfavourable at roughly 30–33 times forward earnings — survives independent verification against ABB's first-quarter 2026 primary release (22 April 2026). Every load-bearing figure checks out, and the sum-of-the-parts fair value is built on current, post-guidance-raise financials rather than a stale anchor.
skeptic_corrections: (1) Margin quality: the headline 23.5% operational earnings margin is materially flattered. Per ABB's first-quarter 2026 release, margin expanded 320 basis points year on year, of which 250 basis points came from the net real-estate gain on sale and only roughly 70 basis points reflected underlying business performance. The bull thesis and the forensic dossier note the $425 million free-cash-flow one-off but understate that it also dominates the reported margin beat. Underlying margin is closer to roughly 21% — at the top of the 18–22% target range — not a clean 23.5% structural over-delivery. (2) Valuation: the dossier cites roughly 33 times forward and roughly 39 times trailing. Current third-party data (GuruFocus, 19 June 2026) shows a forward price-to-earnings ratio of roughly 30.6 times — slightly less extreme than stated, but the 'rich, no discount to capture' conclusion stands (still roughly 46% above the industrial-products median; GF Value of $63.66 flags the stock as significantly overvalued). (3) The NVIDIA tie is correctly characterised as an architecture and specification white paper (October 2025), not a binding dollar-value supply award. The 800-volt DC rollout is targeted for 2027, so the revenue it represents is optionality, not contracted business.
skeptic_evidence: ABB Q1 2026 results (Apr 22 2026): record orders >$11.3B (+24% comparable), book-to-bill 1.29, backlog $27.5B (+22%), revenue $8.7B (+18%/+11% comp), operational EBITA margin 23.5% — of the 320bps margin gain, 250bps from real-estate sale and ~70bps from business performance; FCF $1.25B (+92%) incl. $425M real-estate gain; FY26 comparable-revenue guide raised to high-single/low-double-digit (new.abb.com/news/detail/135137; ABB-Q1-2026-press-release-English.pdf).

ABB to divest Robotics to SoftBank for $5.375B headline, ~$5.3B net cash + ~$2.4B pre-tax book gain, closing mid-to-late 2026 subject to EU/China/US regulatory approval (new.abb.com/news/detail/129685, announced Oct 8 2025; group.softbank/en/news/press/20251008).

ABB–NVIDIA 800 VDC collaboration: 'Redefining Power Infrastructure for AI' white paper, Oct 2025; reference designs / modular power blocks for 1-MW racks, 800V rollout targeted 2027 — an ecosystem/spec-in partnership, not a disclosed-dollar supply award (new.abb.com/news/detail/129788; resources.news.e.abb.com ABB_800_VDC_NVIDIA_white_paper.pdf).

Valuation: ABBNY forward P/E ~30.6x (GuruFocus, Jun 19 2026), ~46% above industrial-products median (~21x); GF Value $63.66 — flagged significantly overvalued; price $108.47 (gurufocus.com/term/forward-pe-ratio/ABBNY; stockanalysis.com/quote/otc/ABBNY).

scale.role: Global electrification/switchgear incumbent; NVDA 800VDC spec-in, record Q1 orders, FY26 guide raise.
scale.rationale: Quality global-electrification franchise (record >$11.3B orders, book-to-bill 1.29, very diversified) — a solid LEVER; the diversification cushions an AI air-pocket but also dilutes the AI bolt, and the NVDA tie is an architecture spec-in, not a binding award. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS with a material margin-quality fix (the 23.5% EBITA margin is flattered ~250bps by a real-estate gain; underlying ~21%), so a ~10pt haircut off a ~60 franchise. Catalyst FIRED into a +87% move; symmetric-to-unfavorable, wait.
scale.demand_durability: High — global electrification, very diversified (cushions an AI air-pocket but dilutes the AI bolt).
